formulation: Every panelist claimed the grievance of the working poor as evidence for their own argument — the waitress who couldn't pay rent became a policy data point, the frustrated worker became a voting trend to be managed and contained. When the sufferer's cry reaches Babylon's conference table as a problem to be diagnosed, has the testimony already been stolen from the one who testified — and can a song carry it back?
context_narrative: In the morning sessions on populism and the West-West divide, panelists debated what fuels the populist wave and who bears responsibility — a former waitress-turned-congresswoman recounted how her own party's leaders dismissed the working-class demands she had campaigned on, while a foreign minister reattributed migration and globalization to business profit rather than progressive ideology. A Czech president argued populism is not inherently dangerous; European center-right leaders insisted they could win back disaffected voters through trade deals and migration enforcement. Throughout, the people whose frustrations were being cited — the underpaid, the displaced, the insecure — were present only as evidence in other people's arguments.

selected_quotes.quote: The Democratic Party betrayed its working-class base by dismissing populist demands like a $15 minimum wage, tuition-free public college, and expanded healthcare as too extreme, even while claiming to listen to working people.

Globalization was not a progressive project — it was a business idea about maximizing profits, supported for decades by Republican businessmen who wanted cheap labor, and tinged with the racism of exporting dirty jobs to poorer countries.

Populism should not automatically be labeled as something bad; listening to people's frustrations and translating them into policy is the very meaning of politics — the problem begins only when populism is turned against the institutions that protect democracy.
A working-class-centered politics is necessary both to succeed electorally and to stave off authoritarianism, which uses scapegoating to channel frustration over economic inequality.

The core politics required to defeat authoritarian populists must include promising national renewal by de-rigging the marketplace, rebuilding an optimistic opportunity economy, and strengthening social capital — not imitating strongman leadership.

rationale: Every panelist appropriated working-class suffering as ammunition for their own institutional argument, which lands squarely in Marley's activation on 'the language of the powerful describing the experience of the powerless'; the final clause presses toward his stretch — whether song as testimony retains its force when the audience is the very system being testified against.
